Default: 17.\n\n### Arguments\n\n- `x`: One or more expressions or columns (Tuple, Array, Date, DateTime, String, or numeric types).\n\n### Returns\n\nThe approximate number of distinct values as a `UInt64`.\n\n### Example\n\n```sql\nSELECT uniqCombined(number) FROM numbers(1e6)\n```\n\nResult:\n\n```result\n┌─uniqCombined(number)─┐\n│              1001148 │\n└──────────────────────┘\n```\n\n## uniqCombined64\n\nCalculates the approximate number of distinct argument values, similar to `uniqCombined`, but uses a 64-bit hash for all data types. This provides higher accuracy for very large cardinalities, especially for non-string types.\n\n### Syntax\n\n```sql\nuniqCombined64(HLL_precision)(x[, ...])\n```\n\n### Parameters\n\n- `HLL_precision`: An optional `UInt64` (base-2 logarithm of HyperLogLog cells). Default: 17.\n\n### Arguments\n\n- `x`: One or more expressions or columns (Tuple, Array, Date, DateTime, String, or numeric types).\n\n### Returns\n\nThe approximate number of distinct values as a `UInt64`.\n\n### Example\n\n```sql\nSELECT uniqCombined64(number) FROM numbers(1e6)\n```\n\nResult:\n\n```result\n┌─uniqCombined64(number)─┐\n│                1000000 │\n└────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n## uniqExact\n\nCalculates the exact number of distinct argument values. This function consumes `O(n)` memory, where `n` is the number of distinct values, and should be used when absolute precision is required.\n\n### Syntax\n\n```sql\nuniqExact(x[, ...])\n```\n\n### Arguments\n\n- `x`: One or more expressions or columns (Tuple, Array, Date, DateTime, String, or numeric types).\n\n### Returns\n\nThe exact number of distinct values as a `UInt64`.\n\n### Example\n\n```sql\nSELECT uniqExact(number % 10) FROM numbers(100)\n```\n\nResult:\n\n```result\n┌─uniqExact(modulo(number, 10))─┐\n│                            10 │\n└───────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n## uniqHLL12\n\nCalculates the approximate number of distinct argument values using the HyperLogLog algorithm with 2^12 5-bit cells. This function is generally not recommended for new development due to its lower accuracy compared to `uniq` or `uniqCombined`.\n\n### Syntax\n\n```sql\nuniqHLL12(x[, ...])\n```\n\n### Arguments\n\n- `x`: One or more expressions or columns (Tuple, Array, Date, DateTime, String, or numeric types).\n\n### Returns\n\nThe approximate number of distinct values as a `UInt64`.\n\n### Example\n\n```sql\nSELECT uniqHLL12(number) FROM numbers(1000)\n```\n\nResult:\n\n```result\n┌─uniqHLL12(number)─┐\n│               999 │\n└───────────────────┘\n```\n\n## uniqTheta\n\nCalculates the approximate number of distinct argument values using the Theta Sketch Framework (KMV algorithm). It provides a relative error of 3.125% (95% confidence).\n\n### Syntax\n\n```sql\nuniqTheta(x[, ...])\n```\n\n### Arguments\n\n- `x`: One or more expressions or columns (Tuple, Array, Date, DateTime, String, or numeric types).\n\n### Returns\n\nThe approximate number of distinct values as a `UInt64`.\n\n### Example\n\n```sql\nSELECT uniqTheta(number) FROM numbers(1000)\n```\n\nResult:\n\n```result\n┌─uniqTheta(number)─┐\n│              1000 │\n└───────────────────┘\n```\n\n## varPop\n\nCalculates the population variance of a set of numeric values. This function uses a numerically unstable algorithm.\n\n### Syntax\n\n```sql\nvarPop(x)\n```\n\nAlias: `VAR_POP`\n\n### Arguments\n\n- `x`: A numeric expression (Integer, Float, or Decimal).\n\n### Returns\n\nThe population variance as a `Float64`.\n\n### Example\n\n```sql\nSELECT varPop(number) FROM numbers(10)\n```\n\nResult:\n\n```result\n┌─varPop(number)─┐\n│           8.25 │\n└────────────────┘\n```\n\n## varPopStable\n\nCalculates the population variance of a set of numeric values using a numerically stable algorithm. This function is generally slower but provides more accurate results than `varPop`.\n\n### Syntax\n\n```sql\nvarPopStable(x)\n```\n\nAlias: `VAR_POP_STABLE`\n\n### Arguments\n\n- `x`: A numeric expression (Integer, Float, or Decimal).\n\n### Returns\n\nThe population variance as a `Float64`.\n\n### Example\n\n```sql\nSELECT varPopStable(number) FROM numbers(10)\n```\n\nResult:\n\n```result\n┌─varPopStable(number)─┐\n│                 8.25 │\n└──────────────────────┘\n```\n\n## varSamp\n\nCalculates the sample variance of a set of numeric values. This function uses a numerically unstable algorithm.\n\n### Syntax\n\n```sql\nvarSamp(x)\n```\n\nAlias: `VAR_SAMP`\n\n### Arguments\n\n- `x`: A numeric expression (Integer, Float, or Decimal).\n\n### Returns\n\nThe sample variance as a `Float64`.\n\n### Example\n\n```sql\nSELECT varSamp(number) FROM numbers(10)\n```\n\nResult:\n\n```result\n┌─varSamp(number)─┐\n│            9.166666666666666 │\n└─────────────────┘\n```\n\n## varSampStable\n\nCalculates the sample variance of a set of numeric values using a numerically stable algorithm. This function is generally slower but provides more accurate results than `varSamp`.\n\n### Syntax\n\n```sql\nvarSampStable(x)\n```\n\nAlias: `VAR_SAMP_STABLE`\n\n### Arguments\n\n- `x`: A numeric expression (Integer, Float, or Decimal).\n\n### Returns\n\nThe sample variance as a `Float64`.\n\n### Example\n\n```sql\nSELECT varSampStable(number) FROM numbers(10)\n```\n\nResult:\n\n```result\n┌─varSampStable(number)─┐\n│    9.166666666666666 │\n└───────────────────────┘\n```\n\n## welchTTest\n\nApplies Welch's t-test to compare two samples and determine if their population means are equal, without assuming equal variances.\n\n### Syntax\n\n```sql\nwelchTTest([confidence_level])(sample_data, sample_index)\n```\n\n### Parameters\n\n- `confidence_level`: An optional `Float64` confidence level for calculating confidence intervals.\n\n### Arguments\n\n- `sample_data`: The numeric values from both samples (Integer, Float, or Decimal).\n- `sample_index`: An `Integer` (0 or 1) indicating which sample the `sample_data` belongs to.\n\n### Returns\n\nA tuple containing the calculated t-statistic and p-value. If `confidence_level` is specified, it also includes the lower and upper bounds of the confidence interval. Tuple(Float64, Float64) or Tuple(Float64, Float64, Float64, Float64).\n\n### Example\n\n```sql\nWITH welch_ttest AS (\n  SELECT arrayJoin([(20.3,0), (22.1,0), (21.9,0), (18.9,1), (20.3,1), (19.0,1)]) AS data_pair\n)\nSELECT welchTTest(data_pair.1, data_pair.2) FROM welch_ttest\n```\n\nResult:\n\n```result\n┌─welchTTest(data_pair.1, data_pair.2)──────┐\n│ (2.7988719532211235,0.051807360348581945) │\n└───────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"8:[\"$\",\"div\",null,{\"className\":\"relative max-w-7xl mx-auto px-5 md:px-6 xl:grid xl:grid-cols-[1fr_280px]\",\"children\":[[\"$\",\"div\",null,{\"className\":\"max-w-4xl text-body md:px-4 md:pb-20 relative\",\"role\":\"main\",\"children\":[[\"$\",\"$L20\",null,{\"pageContent\":\"$21\",\"pagePath\":\"app/__sql-reference/functions/aggregate-functions\",\"children\":[\"$\",\"article\",null,{\"children\":[[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":1,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"Aggregate functions\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Aggregate functions perform a calculation on a set of values and return a single value. They are commonly used in data analysis and reporting to summarize large datasets. These functions can be used to compute sums, averages, counts, and other statistical measures.\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":[\"During aggregation, \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"NULL\"}],\" arguments are generally skipped. If an aggregate function has multiple arguments, any row where one or more of these arguments are \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"NULL\"}],\" will be ignored. Some functions, like \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"first_value\"}],\" or \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"last_value\"}],\" with the \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"RESPECT NULLS\"}],\" modifier, are exceptions to this rule.\"]}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":2,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"Aggregate function combinators\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"The name of an aggregate function can have a suffix appended to it. This changes the way the aggregate function works.\"}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":3,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"-If\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"The suffix -If can be appended to the name of any aggregate function. In this case, the aggregate function accepts an extra argument – a condition (Uint8 type). The aggregate function processes only the rows that trigger the condition. If the condition was not triggered even once, it returns a default value (usually zeros or empty strings).\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":[\"Examples: \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"sumIf(column, cond)\"}],\", \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"countIf(cond)\"}],\", \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"avgIf(x, cond)\"}],\", \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"quantilesTimingIf(level1, level2)(x, cond)\"}],\", \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"argMinIf(arg, val, cond)\"}],\" and so on.\"]}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":[\"With conditional aggregate functions, you can calculate aggregates for several conditions at once, without using subqueries and \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"JOIN\"}],\"s. For example, conditional aggregate functions can be used to implement the segment comparison functionality.\"]}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":3,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"-Array\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"The -Array suffix can be appended to any aggregate function. In this case, the aggregate function takes arguments of the 'Array(T)' type (arrays) instead of 'T' type arguments. If the aggregate function accepts multiple arguments, this must be arrays of equal lengths. When processing arrays, the aggregate function works like the original aggregate function across all array elements.\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":[\"Example 1: \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"sumArray(arr)\"}],\" - Totals all the elements of all 'arr' arrays. In this example, it could have been written more simply: \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"sum(arraySum(arr))\"}],\".\"]}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":[\"Example 2: \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"uniqArray(arr)\"}],\" – Counts the number of unique elements in all 'arr' arrays. This could be done an easier way: \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"uniq(arrayJoin(arr))\"}],\", but it's not always possible to add 'arrayJoin' to a query.\"]}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":[\"-If and -Array can be combined. However, 'Array' must come first, then 'If'. Examples: \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"uniqArrayIf(arr, cond)\"}],\", \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"quantilesTimingArrayIf(level1, level2)(arr, cond)\"}],\". Due to this order, the 'cond' argument won't be an array.\"]}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":3,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"-Map\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"The -Map suffix can be appended to any aggregate function. This will create an aggregate function which gets Map type as an argument, and aggregates values of each key of the map separately using the specified aggregate function. The result is also of a Map type.\"}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":4,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"Example\"}],\"$L23\",[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Result:\"}],\"$L24\",[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":3,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"-SimpleState\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"If you apply this combinator, the aggregate function returns the same value but with a different type. This is a SimpleAggregateFunction(...) that can be stored in a table to work with AggregatingMergeTree tables.\"}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":4,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"Syntax\"}],\"$L25\",[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":4,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"Arguments\"}],[\"$\",\"ul\",null,{\"children\":[\"$\",\"li\",null,{\"children\":[[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"x\"}],\": Aggregate function parameters.\"]}]}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":4,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"Returned values\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":[\"The value of an aggregate function with the \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"SimpleAggregateFunction(...)\"}],\" type.\"]}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":4,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"Example\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Query:\"}],\"$L26\",[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Result:\"}],\"$L27\",[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":3,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"-State\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":[\"If you apply this combinator, the aggregate function doesn't return the resulting value (such as the number of unique values for the uniq function), but an intermediate state of the aggregation (for \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"uniq\"}],\", this is the hash table for calculating the number of unique values). This is an \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"AggregateFunction(...)\"}],\" that can be used for further processing or stored in a table to finish aggregating later.\"]}],[\"$\",\"div\",null,{\"className\":\"my-4 flex items-start gap-2 py-3 px-4 rounded border border-v-border bg-v-callout-info combinator.\"}]]}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":3,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"-Merge\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"If you apply this combinator, the aggregate function takes the intermediate aggregation state as an argument, combines the states to finish aggregation, and returns the resulting value.\"}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":3,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"-MergeState\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Merges the intermediate aggregation states in the same way as the -Merge combinator. However, it doesn't return the resulting value, but an intermediate aggregation state, similar to the -State combinator.\"}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":3,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"-ForEach\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":[\"Converts an aggregate function for tables into an aggregate function for arrays that aggregates the corresponding array items and returns an array of results. For example, \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"sumForEach\"}],\" for the arrays \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"[1, 2]\"}],\", \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"[3, 4, 5]\"}],\"and\",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"[6, 7]\"}],\"returns the result \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"[10, 13, 5]\"}],\" after adding together the corresponding array items.\"]}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":3,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"-Distinct\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":[\"Every unique combination of arguments will be aggregated only once. Repeating values are ignored.\",\" \",\"Examples: \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"sum(DISTINCT x)\"}],\" (or \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"sumDistinct(x)\"}],\"), \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"groupArray(DISTINCT x)\"}],\" (or \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"groupArrayDistinct(x)\"}],\"), \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"corrStable(DISTINCT x, y)\"}],\" (or \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"corrStableDistinct(x, y)\"}],\") and so on.\"]}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":3,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"-OrDefault\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Changes behavior of an aggregate function.\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"If an aggregate function doesn't have input values, with this combinator it returns the default value for its return data type. Applies to the aggregate functions that can take empty input data.\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":[[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"-OrDefault\"}],\" can be used with other combinators.\"]}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":4,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"Syntax\"}],\"$L28\",[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":4,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"Arguments\"}],[\"$\",\"ul\",null,{\"children\":[\"$\",\"li\",null,{\"children\":[[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"x\"}],\": Aggregate function parameters.\"]}]}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":4,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"Returned values\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Returns the default value of an aggregate function's return type if there is nothing to aggregate.\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Type depends on the aggregate function used.\"}],[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":4,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"Example\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Query:\"}],\"$L29\",[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Result:\"}],\"$L2a\",[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":[\"Also \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"-OrDefault\"}],\" can be used with another combinators. It is useful when the aggregate function doesn't accept the empty input.\"]}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Query:\"}],\"$L2b\",[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Result:\"}],\"$L2c\",[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":3,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"-OrNull\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Changes behavior of an aggregate function.\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"This combinator converts a result of an aggregate function to the Nullable data type. Groups are created by splitting the values from one column into intervals.\"}],\"$L32\",[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":4,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"Arguments\"}],[\"$\",\"ul\",null,{\"children\":[[\"$\",\"li\",null,{\"children\":[[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"start\"}],\": Starting value of the whole required interval for \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"resampling_key\"}],\" values.\"]}],[\"$\",\"li\",null,{\"children\":[[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"stop\"}],\": Ending value of the whole required interval for \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"resampling_key\"}],\" values. Since we use integer representation for age, we get ages in the \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"[30, 59]\"}],\" and \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"[60,74]\"}],\" intervals.\"]}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":[\"To aggregate names in an array, we use the groupArray aggregate function. It takes one argument. In our case, it's the \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"name\"}],\" column. The \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"groupArrayResample\"}],\" function should use the \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"age\"}],\" column to aggregate names by age. To define the required intervals, we pass the \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"30, 75, 30\"}],\" arguments into the \",[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"groupArrayResample\"}],\" function.\"]}],\"$L34\",[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Result:\"}],\"$L35\",[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Consider the results.\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":[[\"$\",\"code\",null,{\"children\":\"John\"}],\" is out of the sample because he's too young. Other people are distributed according to the specified age intervals.\"]}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Now let's count the total number of people and their average wage in the specified age intervals.\"}],\"$L36\",[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"Result:\"}],\"$L37\",[\"$\",\"$L22\",null,{\"level\":3,\"name\":\"Heading\",\"children\":\"-ArgMin\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"children\":\"The suffix -ArgMin can be appended to the name of any aggregate function.
